question: What are the two modes of locks discussed for lock-based protocols, and what actions does each mode permit?
mock_answer: The two primary lock modes are Shared and Exclusive. If a transaction holds a shared-mode lock (S) on a data item, it can read but not write to that item. If it holds an exclusive-mode lock (X), it can both read and write the item.
rubric: Must identify the two lock modes as Shared and Exclusive.
Must state that a Shared lock permits reading but not writing.
Must state that an Exclusive lock permits both reading and writing.
gold_chunks: There are various modes in which a data item may be locked.
In this section, we restrict our attention to two modes:
1.  Shared . If a transaction T i has obtained a sharedmode lock (denoted by S) on item Q, then T i can read, but cannot write, Q.
2.  Exclusive . If a transaction T i has obtained an exclusive-mode lock (denoted by X) on item Q , then T i can both read and write Q .

question: What are the two phases of the two-phase locking protocol?
mock_answer: The two-phase locking protocol divides a transaction's execution into two distinct phases. The first is the 'growing phase,' during which the transaction may obtain locks but is not allowed to release any. The second is the 'shrinking phase,' which begins once the transaction releases its first lock; in this phase, the transaction may release locks but cannot obtain any new ones.
rubric: Must identify the two phases as the growing phase and the shrinking phase.
Must describe the growing phase as a period for obtaining locks without releasing any.
Must describe the shrinking phase as a period for releasing locks without obtaining new ones.
gold_chunks: This protocol requires that each transaction issue lock and unlock requests in two phases:
1. Growing phase . A transaction may obtain locks, but may not release any lock.
2. Shrinking phase . A transaction may release locks, but may not obtain any new locks.
Once the transaction releases a lock, it enters the shrinking phase, and it can issue no more lock requests.

question: What is the difference between the strict two-phase locking protocol and the rigorous two-phase locking protocol?
mock_answer: Both are variants of two-phase locking that hold locks until a transaction commits. The strict two-phase locking protocol requires that all exclusive-mode locks acquired by a transaction are held until that transaction commits. The rigorous two-phase locking protocol is more restrictive, requiring that all locks, including both shared and exclusive-mode locks, be held until the transaction commits.
rubric: Must state that strict two-phase locking requires holding all exclusive-mode locks until commit.
Must state that rigorous two-phase locking requires holding all locks (both shared and exclusive) until commit.
Must correctly identify rigorous two-phase locking as the more restrictive of the two.
gold_chunks: Cascading rollbacks can be avoided by a modification of two-phase locking called the strict two-phase locking protocol .
This protocol requires not only that locking be two phase, but also that all exclusive-mode locks taken by a transaction be held until that transaction commits.
Another variant of two-phase locking is the rigorous twophase locking protocol , which requires that all locks be held until the transaction commits.

question: Explain the problem of starvation when granting locks and describe the mechanism used to prevent it.
mock_answer: Starvation is a situation where a transaction may never make progress because its lock request is perpetually denied. For example, if a transaction T1 requests an exclusive lock on an item, it must wait for any existing shared locks to be released. If a continuous stream of new transactions request and are granted shared locks on that same item, T1's request for an exclusive lock might never be granted. This can be avoided by managing lock requests in a queue. A transaction Ti's lock request on an item Q is granted only if there are no conflicting locks on Q AND there is no other transaction that requested a lock on Q before Ti and is still waiting. This ensures a lock request will never be blocked by a request that is made later.
rubric: Must define starvation as a state where a transaction's lock request is never granted.
Must provide an example, such as an exclusive lock request being repeatedly blocked by a stream of shared lock requests.
Must explain that the solution involves ensuring earlier requests are not blocked by later ones.
Must state the two conditions for granting a lock: no conflicting locks are held, and no earlier transaction is waiting for a lock on the same item.
gold_chunks: Suppose a transaction T 2  has a shared-mode lock on a data item, and another transaction T 1  requests an exclusive-mode lock on the data item.
T 1  has to wait for T 2 to release the sharedmode lock.
Meanwhile, a transaction T 3 may request a shared-mode lock on the same data item.
The lock request is compatible with the lock granted to T 2 , so T 3  may be granted the shared-mode lock.

In fact, it is possible that there is a sequence of transactions that each requests a sharedmode lock on the data item, and each transaction releases the lock a short while after it is granted, but T 1  never gets the exclusive-mode lock on the data item.
The transaction T 1  may never make progress, and is said to be starved .

We can avoid starvation of transactions by granting locks in the following manner: When a transaction T i requests a lock on a data item Q in a particular mode M, the concurrency-control manager grants the lock provided that:
- There is no other transaction holding a lock on Q in a mode that conflicts with M .
- There is no other transaction that is waiting for a lock on Q and that made its lock request before T i .
Thus, a lock request will never get blocked by a lock request that is made later.

composites: - There is no other transaction holding a lock on Q in a mode that conflicts with M .
- There is no other transaction that is waiting for a lock on Q and that made its lock request before T i .

question: Compare the two-phase locking protocol with the tree-locking protocol, highlighting one key advantage and one key disadvantage of each.
mock_answer: The tree-locking protocol has a major advantage over two-phase locking in that it is deadlock-free, meaning transaction rollbacks due to deadlock are not required. However, its disadvantage is that a transaction may be forced to lock data items it does not actually access, simply to follow the tree structure to a desired node. This can increase locking overhead and reduce concurrency. Conversely, while the two-phase locking protocol does not require locking unaccessed data, its primary disadvantage is that it does not ensure freedom from deadlock, which can occur if transactions wait for each other's locks in a cycle.
rubric: Must state that the tree-locking protocol's advantage is being deadlock-free.
Must state that the tree-locking protocol's disadvantage is potentially locking unaccessed data items.
Must state that the two-phase locking protocol's disadvantage is its susceptibility to deadlock.
gold_chunks: Two-phase locking does not ensure freedom from deadlock.
The tree-locking protocol has an advantage over the two-phase locking protocol in that, unlike twophase locking, it is deadlock-free, so no rollbacks are required.
However, the protocol has the disadvantage that, in some cases, a transaction may have to lock data items that it does not access.
This additional locking results in increased locking overhead, the possibility of additional waiting time, and a potential decrease in concurrency.

question: What is a lock conversion, and how can it improve concurrency under the two-phase locking protocol?
mock_answer: A lock conversion is a mechanism to change the mode of a lock a transaction already holds, such as upgrading a shared lock to an exclusive lock or downgrading an exclusive lock to a shared one. This can improve concurrency by allowing a transaction to hold a less restrictive lock for as long as possible. For example, a transaction that reads several data items and then writes to just one of them can initially acquire a shared lock on that item. This allows other transactions to read it concurrently. Only when the transaction is ready to perform the write does it upgrade its lock to exclusive mode. This allows for more simultaneous access compared to taking an exclusive lock from the beginning, which would have blocked all other transactions.
rubric: Must define lock conversion as upgrading or downgrading a lock's mode.
Must explain that it improves concurrency by holding less restrictive locks for longer.
Must provide an example, such as initially holding a shared lock for reading and later upgrading to an exclusive lock for writing.
gold_chunks: This observation leads us to a refinement of the basic twophase locking protocol, in which lock conversions are allowed.
We shall provide a mechanism for upgrading a shared lock to an exclusive lock, and downgrading an exclusive lock to a shared lock.
Notice, however, that T 8  needs an exclusive lock on a 1  only at the end of its execution, when it writes a 1 .

Thus, if T 8  could initially lock a 1  in shared mode, and then could later change the lock to exclusive mode, we could get more concurrency, since T 8  and T 9  could access a 1 and a 2  simultaneously.

question: A database system uses the wait-die deadlock prevention scheme, where each transaction is assigned a unique timestamp upon starting. An older transaction T_old (with a smaller timestamp) requests a data item held by a younger transaction T_young (with a larger timestamp). In a separate scenario, T_young requests a data item held by T_old. Based on the rules of the wait-die scheme, what happens in each case, and why does this scheme prevent deadlock cycles from forming?
mock_answer:
In the wait-die scheme, the outcome of a lock conflict is determined by comparing the timestamps of the transactions involved. 
1. When the older transaction, T_old, requests an item held by the younger T_young, T_old is allowed to wait. This is because its timestamp is smaller than T_young's.
2. When the younger transaction, T_young, requests an item held by the older T_old, T_young is rolled back (it "dies"). This is because its timestamp is not smaller than T_old's.
This scheme prevents deadlocks because a wait can only occur in one direction: an older transaction waiting for a younger one. A deadlock requires a cyclic wait, such as T1 waiting for T2, and T2 waiting for T1. In the wait-die scheme, this would mean T1 is older than T2, and T2 is older than T1, which is a logical impossibility. Since waits only flow from older to younger transactions, a cycle can never form.
rubric: Must correctly state that the older transaction (T_old) waits for the younger one (T_young).
Must correctly state that the younger transaction (T_young) is rolled back when requesting a resource from the older one (T_old).
Must reason that this prevents deadlock by ensuring that waits only happen in one direction (older for younger).
Must explain that a cycle of waits is impossible because it would require a transaction to be simultaneously older and younger than another, which contradicts the nature of unique timestamps.
gold_chunks: To control the preemption, we assign a unique timestamp, based on a counter or on the system clock, to each transaction when it begins.
The wait-die scheme is a nonpreemptive technique.
When transaction T i requests a data item currently held by T j , T i is allowed to wait only if it has a timestamp smaller than that of T j (i.e., T i is older than T j ).
Otherwise, T i is rolled back (dies).

composites: When transaction T i requests a data item currently held by T j , T i is allowed to wait only if it has a timestamp smaller than that of T j (i.e., T i is older than T j ).
Otherwise, T i is rolled back (dies).
